title: 6 Best Mercari Alternatives in 2025
metaDescription: Looking for Mercari alternatives with better fees? Here are 6 platforms that outperform Mercari in 2025—starting with one that caps fees at $20.
heroImageAlt: A flat lay of various items for sale including electronics, clothing, and books arranged on a wooden surface next to a smartphone
content:
6 Best Mercari Alternatives in 2025

Mercari deserves credit for making secondhand selling feel approachable. The app is clean, the listing process is simple, and the no transaction fee is easy to understand. But simple doesn't always mean best — and once you hit the platform's limitations (no fee cap, buyer service fees that complicate pricing, occasional slow dispute resolution), it's worth knowing what else is out there. Here are six Mercari alternatives worth considering in 2025.

Mercari vs. 6 Alternatives: Side-by-Side Comparison

Platform Fees Currency Local Pickup Ships Privacy Verdict
Swapedly No fees Swap Bucks (1 SB = $1) Yes Yes No social login required 🏆 Best Overall
eBay 13.25% Cash / card No Yes Account required Best for rare items
Poshmark $2.95 / 20% Cash / card No Yes Account required Best for fashion
Facebook Marketplace 5% shipped / free local Cash / card Yes Yes FB profile required Large local reach
OfferUp 12.9% min $1.99 Cash / card Yes Yes Account required Good local app
Etsy 6.5% + $0.20/listing Cash / card No Yes Account required Best for handmade

#1 Swapedly — The Mercari Alternative With a Fee Cap

Here's the key number: Mercari charges 10% with no ceiling. Swapedly charges no transaction fees at all. Those two structures look identical until you sell anything worth more than $200 — and then they diverge fast. At $300, you pay $30 on Mercari and $20 on Swapedly. At $500, you pay $50 on Mercari and $20 on Swapedly. The $30 difference on that $500 sale is real money.

Beyond the fee structure, Swapedly does something genuinely different: it uses Swap Bucks (SB) as its internal currency. 1 SB equals $1 in value. When you sell something, you earn SB; you spend those SB buying other things on the platform. It's a circular economy model that makes every sale feel productive rather than transactional. Sell a $150 jacket, earn 135 SB (after the no transaction fee), and immediately browse for something you actually want.

Mercari also charges buyers a separate service fee of about 3-4%, which can make your listed price feel misleading to buyers and complicate negotiations. On Swapedly, the fee structure is simpler and entirely on the seller's side — buyers know exactly what they're paying.

New users start with a $40 Swap Bucks gift card — 40 SB, no conditions. Sign up at swapedly.com to claim it. There's also a referral bonus: bring a friend, earn 1 SB.

For power sellers, Swapedly Plus ($9.99/month) gives you 1.5x SB on every sale plus 5 featured listings per month. Do the math: if you're selling $500/month in goods, the 1.5x multiplier adds an extra $50 in SB value for $9.99. That's a solid return on the subscription.

Swapedly Pros

  • no transaction fee with $20 hard cap — better than Mercari above $200
  • No buyer-side service fees
  • Swap Bucks system makes proceeds immediately useful
  • $40 free SB for new users
  • Privacy-first, no social login required
  • Local pickup and shipping both supported

Swapedly Cons

  • Swap Bucks aren't convertible to cash
  • Smaller listing volume in some categories than Mercari

#2 eBay — Best for High-Value or Rare Items

If Mercari's problem is that it doesn't reach enough buyers for premium-priced items, eBay solves that definitively. Its global reach and auction capability mean you can actually discover what the market will pay for something unusual — a vintage piece of kit, an out-of-production item, or collectibles that have real demand beyond your local area.

The 13.25% fee is the worst on this list, and eBay's complexity can feel punishing for casual sellers. But for the right type of item, eBay's buyer pool justifies the overhead. Think of it as the specialist platform you reach for when you have something truly worth maximizing.

eBay Pros

  • Global reach — no platform has more buyers
  • Auction format works well for rare items
  • Strong seller and buyer protection system

eBay Cons

  • 13.25% fee — highest of the group
  • Complex to learn and navigate
  • Returns and disputes favor buyers

#3 Poshmark — The Fashion Seller's Mercari

Poshmark is what Mercari would be if it went deep on fashion and decided margins didn't matter as much as community. The social mechanics — Posh Parties, sharing other sellers' listings, follower systems — create a genuine community around buying and selling clothes.

The 20% commission above $15 is steep. Really steep. On a $100 dress, you net $80. But sellers who invest in the community often report better sales velocity than on Mercari, because the audience is specifically there to buy fashion, not just casually browsing. Whether the trade-off is worth it depends on how much of your inventory is clothing versus general goods.

Poshmark Pros

  • Dedicated, engaged fashion-buying community
  • Social sharing drives discovery
  • Simple all-in shipping label system

Poshmark Cons

  • 20% fee above $15 — very high
  • Fashion and accessories only
  • Requires active community participation to succeed

#4 Facebook Marketplace — The Local Mercari

Where Mercari is almost entirely shipping-focused, Facebook Marketplace balances shipping and local pickup. For items that are too heavy or bulky to ship economically — furniture, large appliances, bikes — Facebook's local section is often the better tool. And for local sales, it's completely free.

The privacy concern is real and worth repeating: every buyer sees your Facebook profile, name, and photo. For many people, that feels fine. For others, it's a meaningful deterrent. If you care about not sharing your personal social media identity with every stranger you sell to, Facebook Marketplace isn't the answer.

Facebook Marketplace Pros

  • Free for local transactions
  • Huge buyer pool, especially for large local items
  • Easy to use, most people already have accounts

Facebook Marketplace Cons

  • Your Facebook identity is fully visible
  • Scam accounts are a persistent issue
  • Cash-only local deals offer no protection

#5 OfferUp — The Local App With Shipping

OfferUp sits in a similar space to Mercari but leans more heavily into local selling. The app is well-designed, the local discovery feed is useful, and TruYou verification adds some trust to local transactions. Where Mercari excels at shipping small items, OfferUp excels at large-item local deals.

The 12.9% shipping fee (minimum $1.99) is higher than Mercari's 10%, which is a bit of a head-scratcher given OfferUp's positioning as a local-first app. For shipped sales, Mercari or Swapedly are the better financial choice.

OfferUp Pros

  • Great local marketplace app design
  • Large user base in most US cities
  • TruYou verification option adds safety

OfferUp Cons

  • 12.9% shipping fee exceeds Mercari and Swapedly
  • Local cash deals have no payment protection
  • No fee cap

#6 Etsy — Best for Handmade and Vintage Sellers

If your items could fit the "handmade, vintage, or craft supplies" category, Etsy's 6.5% transaction fee is attractive — lower than Mercari's 10%. The audience is specifically seeking unique, creative items, so if your listings match that profile, you get organic traffic that Mercari can't replicate.

The $0.20 per-listing fee and the expectation that you produce a steady stream of inventory make Etsy better suited to semi-professional sellers in the right niche rather than casual declutterers. If you're selling one-off used items, Etsy's audience won't find you relevant.

Etsy Pros

  • 6.5% fee — lower than Mercari
  • Built-in audience for creative and vintage goods
  • Strong platform trust and repeat buyer behavior

Etsy Cons

  • $0.20 per listing fee, renewed every 4 months
  • Not suitable for general used goods
  • Saturated in popular categories

Which Mercari Alternative Should You Switch To?

Mercari is genuinely good for what it is — an easy, approachable platform for shipping small-to-medium items. But if fees matter to you (and they should), Swapedly's zero-fee structure saves real money the moment you sell anything over $200. The Swap Bucks currency is a different mindset but a genuinely useful one, especially if you're regularly buying and selling used goods.

For fashion, add Poshmark. For rare items, check eBay. For local bulk items, try Facebook Marketplace or OfferUp. And if you're making handmade goods, Etsy is worth a look. But for general-purpose secondhand selling with the best fee structure in the market? Swapedly is the answer.

Don't forget: you get 40 free Swap Bucks just for joining. Explore more in our eBay alternatives guide and our comprehensive look at the best places to sell stuff online in 2025.
